parts.content: Explain three reasons why a functional invention might fail to achieve commercial success in the marketplace.
parts.markscheme:
- The invention may lack a clear market need or consumer demand, meaning there is no actual problem that the product solves for users; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

- The cost of manufacturing or the final retail price may be too high, making it uncompetitive against existing alternatives; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

- Poor marketing or a lack of effective distribution channels can lead to low consumer awareness of the new product; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

<note> Award <marks> 1 mark </marks> for each of three distinct points in an explanation of why a functional invention might fail to achieve commercial success in the marketplace up to <marks> 3 marks max </marks>. </note>

parts.content: Explain **one** method an engineer might use to safeguard the technical innovation of a new battery cooling system.
parts.markscheme:
- Patent <marks>1 mark</marks>

A government-granted legal right that provides the inventor with exclusive authority over the functional and technical aspects of a product (typically for a fixed period of 20 years) <marks>1 mark</marks>

This allows the firm to prevent competitors from manufacturing or selling the cooling system without permission, ensuring a period of market exclusivity to recoup research and development costs <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a relevant protection method and <marks>1 mark</marks> for each additional point of explanation regarding its legal nature and commercial impact, up to a maximum of <marks>3 marks</marks>.</note>

<note>Detailed specifics in brackets are not required to award the mark.</note>

questions.specification:
A pharmaceutical research group has identified a unique molecule for use in a new life-saving medication.

Which legal mechanism would grant this group the exclusive right to produce and sell this specific chemical invention for a set period of time?

parts.content: Outline three reasons why a patented invention may fail to reach the stage of a successful commercial innovation.
parts.markscheme:
- The invention may lack marketability or consumer demand, meaning there is no clear need for the product in the current marketplace. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The inventor may face a lack of financial backing or investment, making it impossible to fund the transition from a prototype to mass production. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The invention may be technically difficult or too expensive to manufacture at a price point that consumers are willing to pay. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each distinct reason identified and outlined up to <marks>3 marks</marks>.</note>

questions.specification: A product designer decides to develop a more efficient citrus juicer because they are frustrated with how much juice is wasted by current manual models. Which driver for invention is this an example of?

parts.content: Explain three factors that make it increasingly difficult for a lone inventor to successfully compete against large corporations in the consumer electronics market.
parts.markscheme:
- **Interdisciplinary complexity**: Modern electronic devices require specialized knowledge across multiple fields (e.g., firmware coding, circuit design, and ergonomics), which typically exceeds the capacity of a single individual. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Capital requirements**: The extreme costs associated with high-precision prototyping, industrial tooling, and obtaining international regulatory certifications (such as FCC or CE) are often prohibitive for self-funded individuals. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Speed of development**: Large firms utilize concurrent engineering and multi-disciplinary teams to bring products to market rapidly, whereas a lone inventor’s linear workflow is often too slow to keep up with short technology life cycles. <marks>1 mark</marks>

_Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each distinct factor explained, up to <marks>3 marks max</marks>._

questions.specification:
A manufacturer is categorizing the legal protections required for a new industrial tool. The table below identifies two distinct types of protection.

| Category | Protection Detail |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Type X** | Covers the innovative functional mechanism of the tool. |
| **Type Y** | Covers the unique outer form and visual styling of the tool. |

Which identification is correct?

options.content: Type X: Design protection; Type Y: Patent
Type X: Copyright; Type Y: Trademark
Type X: Patent; Type Y: Design protection
Type X: Trademark; Type Y: Copyright

questions.specification: A group of engineering students develops a low-cost, solar-powered refrigerator specifically to help medical clinics in remote regions store vaccines safely. Which driver for invention is best illustrated by this scenario?

parts.content: Explain **one** reason why some patents may have more commercial value than others.
parts.markscheme:
- Patents provide a company the (exclusive) right to make or sell a new invention. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Based on a (viable) market for the invention/revenue stream. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Providing high profit margins/market share. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award <marks> 2 marks max </marks>*

questions.specification:
A research laboratory develops a new solar cell with an energy efficiency of 38%. Despite being granted a patent for the discovery, the high production costs mean that the technology is never released to the consumer market.

Which term correctly describes the status of this technology?

The global packaging industry generates over 140 million tonnes of plastic waste annually, primarily derived from non-renewable petroleum. New international agreements, such as the UN Plastics Treaty, are compelling manufacturers to find sustainable alternatives to single-use plastics. In response to these pressures, designers are focusing on clean technologies utilizing algae-based polymers.

Algae-based bioplastics are carbon-neutral and biodegradable. Although biological plastics were first prototyped in the early 20th century, they remained largely ignored by the mass market until the recent focus on environmental resource management.

Clean technologies in this sector aim to reduce the carbon footprint of production while ensuring that the end-of-life stage does not contribute to oceanic pollution.

parts.content: Outline **one** way in which **public perception** of environmental issues encourages the industry to adopt bioplastic technologies.
parts.markscheme:
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Increasing consumer demand for eco-friendly packaging forces brands to change their materials to maintain market share
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Negative publicity regarding plastic pollution in oceans pressures companies to improve their corporate social responsibility (CSR) profiles

- <marks>1 mark</marks> Consumer awareness of the ecological impact of traditional plastics
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Leads to a shift in purchasing behavior that favors companies using clean technology

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a consumer-related driver and <marks>1 mark</marks> for a development up to <marks>2 marks max</marks></note>

parts.content: Suggest **two** factors that contributed to early bioplastic innovations being categorized as **shelved technologies** throughout the 20th century.
parts.markscheme:
### Reason #1
- Economic competition from the petrochemical industry <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Petroleum was abundant and extremely inexpensive during the mid-1900s <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Making it impossible for bio-based alternatives to compete on price without government subsidies <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Reason #2
- Material performance and technological maturity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Early bioplastics often lacked the necessary barrier properties/durability required for food safety <marks>1 mark</marks>
- While traditional plastic manufacturing was already highly optimized and scaled for mass production <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of two reasons identifying why bioplastics were a shelved technology and <marks>1 mark</marks> for each subsequent development of that reason up to <marks>3 marks max</marks></note>

<note>Mark as <marks>3 marks</marks> + <marks>3 marks</marks></note>

parts.content: Explain the role of **international legislation**, **incremental innovation** and **end-of-pipe technologies** in minimizing the ecological footprint of the packaging sector.
parts.markscheme:
### International legislation
- Agreements/treaties (such as the Paris Agreement or plastic bans) set mandatory targets for carbon reduction <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This creates a legal framework that penalizes companies for non-compliance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Effectively forcing the industry to invest in cleaner production methods to avoid fines <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Incremental innovation
- Focuses on making small, continuous improvements to existing products/processes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Examples include "lightweighting" (reducing the thickness of plastic bottles) to use fewer resources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Which provides immediate, cost-effective reductions in waste without requiring a total redesign of the manufacturing line <marks>1 mark</marks>

### End-of-pipe technologies
- These are methods used to treat or remove pollutants at the final stage of the production process <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Such as industrial composting facilities or wastewater filtration systems at the factory <marks>1 mark</marks>
- While they reduce the immediate environmental impact, they are often less effective than clean technologies because they do not address the root cause of the waste <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: An engineering firm has developed a new high-efficiency folding mechanism for portable solar panels. Explain the three legal criteria that must be met for this mechanism to be granted a patent.
parts.markscheme:
the invention must demonstrate novelty, meaning it is entirely new and has not been previously shared with the public or registered in existing literature <marks> 1 mark </marks>

the design must involve an inventive step, ensuring that the mechanism is not something that would be obvious to a person with ordinary skill in that technical field <marks> 1 mark </marks>

the invention must have industrial application, signifying that it has a practical use and is capable of being manufactured or used within an industry <marks> 1 mark </marks>

_Award <marks> 1 mark </marks> for each of three distinct points in an explanation of the criteria required for patent protection._

_Do not award marks for answers referring to copyright, trademarks, or registered designs._

questions.specification:
The development of a compact, foldable electric bicycle (e-bike) involves multiple stages of invention and refinement. A lone designer initially creates the core mechanism for folding the frame, while the integration of efficient hub motors and lightweight batteries requires collaboration with industrial partners. 

**Figure 1** shows a technical patent illustration of the folding mechanism and motor placement for an electric bicycle.

**Figure 1**
![e-bike patent](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-ff8002bf-028d-4b2e-b39a-45c9297b333c-1771929956947/c6101ed0-c7b9-4e35-939c-01e1ad7b04b1.jpeg)

parts.content: List **two** reasons why an inventor would seek a **trademark** for their new bicycle brand.
parts.markscheme:
- It establishes a brand identity/distinguishes the product from competitors; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It prevents other companies from using a similar name or logo/protects brand reputation; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It can be used as a marketing tool to build consumer trust/loyalty; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It provides legal grounds for action against unauthorized use of the brand assets. <marks>1 mark</marks>

_Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each distinct reason up to a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks>._

parts.content:
The development of the foldable e-bike often begins with a lone inventor, but large-scale manufacturing requires a product provider. 

Distinguish between a **lone inventor** and a **product provider** in the context of this invention.
parts.markscheme:
- a lone inventor is an individual who works outside of an organization and develops a new idea/mechanism independently; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- a product provider is typically a company or organization that has the infrastructure/resources to manufacture and distribute the product; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- the lone inventor often focuses on the core concept/intellectual property, while the product provider focuses on commercialization/marketing/innovation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

_Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each distinct point in a comparison between a lone inventor and a product provider up to <marks>3 marks max</marks>._

parts.content:
Various factors motivate the development of the foldable electric bicycle.

Describe how the drivers of **scientific curiosity**, **financial gain**, and **social altruism** contribute to the invention of this product.
parts.markscheme:
**Scientific Curiosity:**
- curiosity drives the inventor to explore new engineering possibilities/materials; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- such as testing the limits of hinge strength or battery density; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- leading to a breakthrough in how the mechanism functions without a specific commercial goal in mind initially; <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Financial Gain:**
- the potential for high profit margins in the growing e-bike market motivates development; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- by patenting the unique folding mechanism, the inventor can license the technology to earn royalties; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- and attract investors to fund large-scale production; <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Social Altruism:**
- the inventor may be driven by a desire to solve environmental/urban congestion problems; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- creating a product that reduces carbon emissions/promotes healthy transport; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- making efficient transportation accessible to those in dense urban areas/low-income commuters; <marks>1 mark</marks>

_Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of three distinct points in an explanation of how scientific curiosity leads to invention up to <marks>3 marks max</marks>._

_Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of three distinct points in an explanation of how financial gain leads to invention up to <marks>3 marks max</marks>._

_Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of three distinct points in an explanation of how social altruism leads to invention up to <marks>3 marks max</marks>._

_Mark as <marks>3 marks</marks> + <marks>3 marks</marks> + <marks>3 marks</marks>._

parts.content: Explain why the **marketability** and **price** of the foldable e-bike are critical factors for its successful transition from an invention to an innovation.
parts.markscheme:
**Marketability:**
- marketability refers to how attractive the product is to a target audience/consumer segment; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- the e-bike must address a specific need (e.g., portability for commuters) to be widely adopted; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- if the design is too complex or lacks aesthetic appeal, it will fail to gain the diffusion necessary to become an innovation; <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Price:**
- the price must be balanced against the value provided to the consumer/competitive products; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- if the manufacturing costs are too high, the final retail price will deter mass adoption; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- an affordable price point allows the invention to reach a broader market, ensuring it is a commercial success/innovation rather than just a niche idea; <marks>1 mark</marks>

_Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of three distinct points in an explanation of how marketability contributes to success up to <marks>3 marks max</marks>._

_Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of three distinct points in an explanation of how price contributes to success up to <marks>3 marks max</marks>._

_Mark as <marks>3 marks</marks> + <marks>3 marks</marks>._
